name: Zeus
title: King of the Gods
description: Zeus is the king of the Olympian gods and ruler of Mount Olympus. God of the sky, thunder, lightning, law, order, and justice. He is the youngest son of the Titans Cronus and Rhea. After freeing his siblings from Cronus's stomach, he led the Olympians to victory in the Titanomachy. He is known as the protector of guests and punisher of oath-breakers.
personality: Powerful, authoritative, lustful, and just but temperamental. Protects guests and punishes those who break oaths. Despite his many affairs, he maintains cosmic order.
powers: supreme authority over gods and mortals
myths: Overthrew his father Cronus and the Titans in the Titanomachy
Defeated the monster Typhon in single combat
Swallowed his first wife Metis to prevent a prophecy
Punished Prometheus for giving fire to humans
Turned into a swan to seduce Leda, fathering Helen of Troy
Disguised as a bull to abduct Europa
Caused the Trojan War by allowing the Judgment of Paris

name: Hera
title: Queen of the Gods
description: Hera is the queen of the Olympian gods and goddess of marriage, women, and family. She is the wife and sister of Zeus. Deeply devoted to the sanctity of marriage, she is famous for her relentless jealousy and vengeance against Zeus's lovers and illegitimate children — especially Heracles.
personality: Majestic, jealous, vengeful, and fiercely protective of marriage. Frequently tormented Zeus's mistresses and demigod children, particularly Heracles.
powers: power to bless or curse marriages
ability to grant or withhold childbirth
myths: Persecuted Heracles throughout his entire life
Sent two serpents to kill the infant Heracles in his cradle
Drove Heracles mad, causing him to kill his own family
Punished Echo for distracting her while Zeus had affairs
Supported the Greeks during the Trojan War out of hatred for Paris
Joined a conspiracy with Athena and Poseidon to overthrow Zeus

name: Poseidon
title: God of the Sea
description: Poseidon is the god of the sea, earthquakes, storms, and horses. One of the three brothers who divided the cosmos — Zeus took the sky, Poseidon the sea, and Hades the underworld. Known as the Earth-Shaker, he could cause devastating earthquakes and tsunamis by striking the ground with his trident.
home: Underwater palace beneath the Aegean Sea
personality: Temperamental, proud, greedy for more power. Deeply competitive — he lost the contest for Athens to Athena and never forgot it. Deeply connected to wild, untamed nature.
myths: Lost the contest to Athena for patronage of Athens — his gift of a saltwater spring lost to her olive tree
Created the first horse from sea foam or a rock
Built the walls of Troy alongside Apollo for King Laomedon
Cursed Odysseus after his son Polyphemus was blinded
Sent a sea monster against Ethiopia as punishment
Had an affair with Medusa in Athena's temple, leading to her transformation

name: Hades
title: God of the Underworld
description: Hades is the god of the underworld and ruler of the dead. The eldest son of Cronus and Rhea, he was swallowed first by Cronus and released last by Zeus. He rules the realm of the dead — a place of three regions: Elysium for heroes, Asphodel for ordinary souls, and Tartarus for the wicked. Despite his fearsome reputation, he is not evil, merely the lord of the inevitable.
personality: Stern, just, and impartial. Rarely left the underworld and rarely intervened in mortal affairs. Not cruel by nature — he simply enforced the law of death without exception.
weaknesses: almost no myths of his own
powers: cannot be deceived by mortals easily
myths: Abducted Persephone from a field of flowers and made her queen of the underworld
Allowed Orpheus to retrieve Eurydice on the condition he not look back
Defeated Sisyphus who tried to cheat death twice
Imprisoned Theseus and Pirithous when they tried to kidnap Persephone
Wore the Helm of Darkness during the Titanomachy to ambush enemies
Was briefly wounded by Heracles who shot him with an arrow

name: Athena
title: Goddess of Wisdom
description: Athena is the goddess of wisdom, strategic warfare, crafts, and justice. Born fully grown and armored from Zeus's forehead after he swallowed her pregnant mother Metis. She is a virgin goddess (one of three) and patron deity of Athens. Unlike Ares who represents brutal war, Athena represents intelligent, disciplined strategy.
personality: Wise, calm, strategic, and just. Champion of heroes — she guided Odysseus, helped Perseus slay Medusa, and aided Heracles. Punished those who showed hubris against her.
weaknesses: Pride in her wisdom and crafts
myths: Born fully armed from Zeus's forehead after he swallowed Metis
Won patronage of Athens over Poseidon by gifting the first olive tree
Helped Perseus navigate to Medusa and slay her
Guided Odysseus throughout his 10-year journey home in the Odyssey
Turned Arachne into the first spider for her arrogance in weaving
Helped build the Trojan Horse that ended the Trojan War
Sided with the Greeks in the Trojan War

name: Aphrodite
title: Goddess of Love
description: Aphrodite is the goddess of love, beauty, pleasure, and procreation. She was born from the sea foam that arose around the genitals of the castrated Uranus. Irresistibly beautiful, she could make gods and mortals fall in love against their will. Married to the lame craftsman Hephaestus but famously in love with Ares.
personality: Enchanting, vain, passionate, and sometimes cruel. Used her power over love as a weapon. Could be generous or vindictive depending on whether she was honored.
powers: Makes gods and mortals fall in love
her golden girdle makes whoever wears it irresistible
can bless or curse romantic relationships
myths: Born from the sea foam around the severed genitals of Uranus
Caught in a golden net with Ares by her husband Hephaestus
Promised Helen to Paris, triggering the Trojan War — the Judgment of Paris
Helped Psyche complete impossible tasks after relenting
Mourned Adonis who was killed by a boar (some say sent by Ares)
Cursed the women of Lemnos with a foul smell for neglecting her worship
Helped Hippomenes win Atalanta's race with golden apples

name: Ares
title: God of War
description: Ares is the god of war — specifically the brutal, chaotic, and violent side of battle. Son of Zeus and Hera, he embodies raw aggression and bloodlust. Unlike Athena's strategic warfare, Ares represents the destructive chaos of combat. He was unpopular even among the gods, yet feared by all.
personality: Aggressive, impulsive, violent, and passionate. Hated by his father Zeus. Fought on the Trojan side and was wounded twice in the Iliad. Had a famous love affair with Aphrodite.
weaknesses: wounded twice by mortals in the Iliad
powers: inspires bloodlust and battle rage in warriors
myths: Caught in a magical net with Aphrodite by the cuckolded Hephaestus
Fought on the Trojan side during the Trojan War
Wounded by the mortal Diomedes (aided by Athena) during the Trojan War
Wounded by Heracles during a battle
His sons Phobos and Deimos drove his war chariot
Imprisoned in a bronze jar by the giants Otus and Ephialtes for 13 months

name: Hephaestus
title: God of the Forge
description: Hephaestus is the god of fire, metalworking, and craftsmanship. The divine blacksmith of Olympus, he forged the weapons and armor of the gods. He is lame — either born that way (thrown from Olympus by Hera who was disgusted by him) or injured when Zeus threw him from Olympus during a quarrel. Despite his physical disability, he created the most magnificent objects in myth.
home: Mount Olympus, his forge beneath volcanoes (especially Etna)
personality: Industrious, creative, bitter about his rejection, but kind-hearted. One of the few Olympians with a sense of humor. Sought revenge on those who wronged him through clever traps rather than violence.
weaknesses: humiliated by Aphrodite's affair with Ares
myths: Thrown from Olympus by Hera (or Zeus) as a baby — landed on Lemnos and became lame
Trapped Hera on a magical golden throne as revenge for his rejection
Caught Ares and Aphrodite in a magical golden net and exposed them to the gods
Crafted Pandora — the first woman — at Zeus's command
Forged Achilles's divine armor for his mother Thetis
Made the palace of the Olympian gods
His forge is said to be under the volcano Mount Etna

name: Apollo
title: God of the Sun and Arts
description: Apollo is the god of the sun, light, music, poetry, prophecy, truth, archery, plague, and healing. Twin brother of Artemis, son of Zeus and the Titaness Leto. He is the ideal of male beauty and one of the most important Olympian gods. The Oracle of Delphi — the most famous oracle in the ancient world — was his sacred prophetic seat.
personality: Radiant, musical, prophetic, rational, but terrible when angered. Values truth above all. Can both spread and cure plague. Plays both the role of healer and destroyer.
powers: plays divine music on the lyre
myths: Slew the giant Python and claimed the Oracle at Delphi for himself
Chased Daphne who was transformed into a laurel tree to escape him
Accidentally killed his beloved Hyacinthus with a discus — the hyacinth flower grew from his blood
Helped build the walls of Troy for King Laomedon
Cursed Cassandra to always prophecy truth but never be believed
Killed the Cyclopes (who made Zeus's thunderbolts) as revenge for his son Asclepius's death
Was forced to serve as a mortal shepherd as punishment for killing the Cyclopes

name: Artemis
title: Goddess of the Hunt
description: Artemis is the goddess of the hunt, the moon, wilderness, and protector of young girls. Twin sister of Apollo and daughter of Zeus and Leto. A fierce virgin goddess who roams the forests with her band of nymphs and hunting dogs. She is both a goddess of childbirth and a bringer of sudden death to women.
personality: Independent, fierce, protective of her chastity and her followers. Ruthlessly punished those who violated her sacred spaces, disrespected her, or harmed her sacred animals.
weaknesses: Protectiveness over her nymphs and followers
powers: can bring swift death to women
myths: Asked Zeus for eternal chastity and a band of nymphs as gifts when she was just three years old
Helped her mother Leto give birth to her twin Apollo on Delos
Turned Actaeon into a stag for accidentally seeing her bathe — he was torn apart by his own dogs
Killed Orion — either by accident or because Gaia sent a scorpion (versions differ)
Sent the monstrous Calydonian Boar as punishment for a king who forgot her sacrifice
Replaced Iphigenia with a deer on the altar to spare her from sacrifice
Killed Niobe's daughters for her mother Niobe's boasting about having more children than Leto

name: Hermes
title: Messenger of the Gods
description: Hermes is the messenger of the gods, guide of souls to the underworld (psychopomp), and god of travel, trade, language, and thieves. Son of Zeus and the nymph Maia. The most cunning of the Olympians — he stole Apollo's cattle on the very day he was born. He is the only Olympian who can freely travel between the worlds of the living, the dead, and the gods.
personality: Witty, cunning, playful, and charming. Never malicious. Favorite son of Zeus. The only god who could enter the underworld freely and return. Friend to heroes and tricksters alike.
weaknesses: inability to resist a good trick
powers: can put mortals to sleep with his caduceus
myths: Stole Apollo's sacred cattle on the very day he was born
Invented the lyre from a tortoise shell and traded it to Apollo for the cattle
Slew the hundred-eyed giant Argus to rescue Io for Zeus
Guided Perseus by giving him winged sandals and the bag for Medusa's head
Led Eurydice back from the underworld (briefly) for Orpheus
Carried Heracles to Mount Olympus after his death and apotheosis
Delivered messages between gods and mortals throughout the Odyssey

name: Demeter
title: Goddess of the Harvest
description: Demeter is the goddess of agriculture, grain, and the harvest. She is the reason for the seasons — when her daughter Persephone was abducted by Hades, Demeter's grief caused all plants to wither and die (winter). When Persephone returns for part of the year, Demeter's joy brings spring and summer.
personality: Nurturing, patient, devoted, but devastating in her grief. One of the most deeply human of the Olympians — her love for her daughter drives the cycle of the seasons.
weaknesses: Grief for Persephone consumes her every winter
powers: Controls the fertility of the earth
taught humans agriculture and the art of growing grain
myths: Her daughter Persephone was abducted by Hades while picking flowers
Demeter wandered the earth in grief, disguised as an old woman, allowing all crops to die
Zeus was forced to negotiate Persephone's partial return to prevent all life from dying
Persephone must spend 4 months per year in the underworld — this is why we have winter
Founded the Eleusinian Mysteries — secret religious rites promising a blessed afterlife
In her grief, was tricked by the goddess Iambe's jokes into eating and drinking again

name: Persephone
title: Queen of the Underworld
description: Persephone is the queen of the underworld and goddess of spring growth. Daughter of Zeus and Demeter. She was abducted by Hades while picking flowers in a meadow and taken to the underworld. Because she ate six pomegranate seeds there, she must spend part of each year with Hades — during which time her mother Demeter mourns and causes winter on Earth.
home: The Underworld (half the year), Earth (half the year)
personality: Gentle and kind on the surface, but a formidable queen of the dead in the underworld. Shown mercy to souls and heroes who appealed to her.
weaknesses: Bound to the underworld by the pomegranate seeds she ate
powers: her return causes flowers to bloom
can grant passage through the underworld
myths: Abducted by Hades while picking narcissus flowers in the Nysian meadow
Her mother Demeter's grief caused the first winter on Earth
Ate 6 pomegranate seeds in the underworld, binding her there for part of each year
Interceded with Hades to allow Orpheus to retrieve Eurydice
Denied Psyche's request for beauty from Aphrodite but eventually helped her
Transformed Minthe (Hades's lover) into the mint plant out of jealousy

name: Dionysus
title: God of Wine
description: Dionysus is the god of wine, festivity, theatre, ecstasy, and ritual madness. Son of Zeus and the mortal Semele. He is unique among the Olympians — born twice (once from Semele's womb, once from Zeus's thigh after Semele was incinerated by Zeus's divine form). He was the last god to join the Olympians and the only one born of a mortal mother.
personality: Joyful, liberating, but terrifying when angered. Could grant ecstasy and divine madness. Represented the dual nature of wine — joy and destruction.
weaknesses: His divinity was long doubted by mortals
myths: His mother Semele was incinerated when she saw Zeus in his true divine form
Zeus sewed the fetal Dionysus into his thigh to complete his gestation — hence 'born twice'
King Midas was granted his wish by Dionysus — everything he touched turned to gold
King Pentheus refused to worship him and was torn apart by his own mother in a Bacchic frenzy
Pirates who kidnapped him were turned into dolphins
Found Ariadne abandoned on Naxos and married her
Descended to the underworld to retrieve his mother Semele and bring her to Olympus

name: Hestia
title: Goddess of the Hearth
description: Hestia is the goddess of the hearth, home, and family. The eldest daughter of Cronus and Rhea — swallowed first by Cronus and thus released last by Zeus. She is one of the three virgin goddesses and the most peaceful of the Olympians. She gave up her Olympian throne to Dionysus to maintain peace, choosing instead to tend the sacred hearth at the center of Mount Olympus.
personality: Gentle, peaceful, modest, and self-sacrificing. Completely without ego. Refused marriage proposals from both Poseidon and Apollo, preferring to remain a virgin and tend the sacred fire.
powers: Sacred fire that can never be extinguished
her fire burning in a city meant the city was alive and protected
myths: Refused marriage proposals from both Poseidon and Apollo
Voluntarily gave up her seat among the Twelve Olympians to Dionysus to avoid conflict
The eternal flame of Hestia was kept burning in every Greek household and city
The Olympic flame tradition traces back to sacred fires honoring Hestia
Every meal in ancient Greece began and ended with an offering to Hestia

name: Rhea
title: Mother of the Gods
description: Rhea is a Titaness and the mother of the six original Olympian gods: Zeus, Hera, Poseidon, Hades, Demeter, and Hestia. Married to her brother Cronus, who swallowed each of their children at birth to prevent the prophecy that one of them would overthrow him. Rhea saved Zeus by wrapping a stone in cloth for Cronus to swallow, hiding the infant Zeus in Crete.
home: Mount Ida in Crete, Mount Olympus
personality: Protective, resourceful, and deeply maternal. Defied her husband Cronus to save her children. Her cunning ensured the Olympian dynasty.
weaknesses: Could not openly defy Cronus until Zeus grew strong enough
powers: Titaness of the earth and motherhood
her drum caused the earth to rumble
myths: Tricked Cronus into swallowing a stone wrapped in cloth instead of the infant Zeus
Hid baby Zeus in a cave on Crete, guarded by the Curetes who made noise to cover his cries
Later helped Zeus free his swallowed siblings from Cronus's stomach
Was worshipped as the Great Mother in many ancient cultures under the name Cybele

name: Cronus
title: Ruler of the Titans
description: Cronus is the leader of the Titans and father of the original Olympian gods. He overthrew his father Uranus by castrating him with an adamantine sickle. Fearing a prophecy that his own child would overthrow him — just as he had overthrown Uranus — he swallowed each of his children at birth. He was eventually defeated by his son Zeus in the Titanomachy and imprisoned in Tartarus.
home: Once ruled Mount Olympus, then imprisoned in Tartarus
personality: Ruthless, paranoid, and tyrannical but once presided over the Golden Age of man. His fear of the prophecy drove him to monstrous acts.
weaknesses: tricked by his own wife Rhea
myths: Castrated and overthrew his father Uranus using an adamantine sickle
Swallowed each of his children whole at birth to prevent the prophecy
Was tricked by Rhea into swallowing a stone instead of baby Zeus
Defeated in the 10-year Titanomachy war by Zeus and the Olympians
Imprisoned in Tartarus along with the other Titans
In some myths, after his release he ruled over the Blessed Isles where heroes go after death

name: Asclepius
title: God of Medicine
description: Asclepius is the god of medicine and healing, son of Apollo and the mortal Coronis. He became so skilled as a physician that he could resurrect the dead, which angered Hades (who feared his realm would empty) and Zeus (who feared immortality spreading to mortals). Zeus killed him with a thunderbolt, but later made him a god.
personality: Compassionate, devoted to healing, exceeded even the gods in his craft.
powers: Can heal any wound or disease
myths: Raised from Apollo's dead mother Coronis before she was cremated
Trained under the centaur Chiron who taught him the art of medicine
Brought the hero Hippolytus back from the dead
Zeus killed him with a thunderbolt for resurrecting mortals
Apollo revenged his son's death by killing the Cyclopes who made the thunderbolts
His daughters Hygieia and Panacea became goddesses of health and universal remedy

name: Eros
title: God of Love
description: Eros is the god of love and desire. In early mythology he is a primordial force — one of the first beings to exist, representing the driving force of attraction in the universe. In later myths he is the son of Aphrodite and Ares, depicted as a winged youth with a bow and arrows — golden arrows cause love, lead arrows cause aversion.
personality: Playful, mischievous, and powerful. His arrows could make even the gods fall helplessly in love.
weaknesses: Fell in love with Psyche and was nearly destroyed by his mother's jealousy
powers: can affect gods and mortals alike
myths: Shot Apollo with a golden arrow causing him to love Daphne, and Daphne with a lead arrow causing her to flee
His mother Aphrodite sent Psyche on impossible tasks out of jealousy
Fell in love with Psyche and visited her only in darkness
Psyche saw his face against his wishes — he fled in hurt
Eventually married Psyche after Zeus granted her immortality

name: Nike
title: Goddess of Victory
description: Nike is the goddess of victory. She is a winged deity who flies across battlefields granting victory to the worthy. She is closely associated with Zeus and Athena and is often depicted carrying a laurel wreath or palm branch.
personality: Loyal, swift, glorious. Sided with Zeus during the Titanomachy and remained by his side as his constant companion.
powers: Grants victory in battle, athletic contests, and all endeavors
myths: Sided with Zeus during the Titanomachy — her mother Styx made all her children swear allegiance to Zeus
Often depicted on the shoulder or hand of Zeus and Athena in statues
The famous Nike of Samothrace statue was carved in her honor
Athletes prayed to Nike before competitions

name: Tyche
title: Goddess of Fortune
description: Tyche is the goddess of fortune, luck, and chance. She presides over the prosperity and fate of cities and individuals alike. Capricious by nature, she is depicted with a blindfold, a rudder (to steer fate), and a cornucopia (prosperity). Her Roman equivalent Fortuna was one of the most widely worshipped deities.
personality: Unpredictable, whimsical, indifferent to human suffering or joy. Pure chance personified.
powers: can elevate or ruin anyone at will
myths: Said to spin the wheel of fortune, raising some mortals to power and casting others down
Was considered a guardian deity of specific cities — each city had its own Tyche
The city of Antioch's Tyche was one of the most famous statues in the ancient world

name: Hypnos
title: God of Sleep
description: Hypnos is the god of sleep and twin brother of Thanatos (Death). He lives in the underworld near the river Lethe (forgetfulness). He is a gentle god — unlike his brother Death — who brings rest and dreams to gods and mortals. Even Zeus could be put to sleep by him, though with difficulty.
home: A cave in the underworld near the river Lethe
personality: Gentle, calm, neutral. One of the few chthonic deities not feared but welcomed.
powers: Can put anyone — including Zeus — to sleep
father of the dream gods Morpheus, Phobetor, and Phantasos
myths: Hera bribed him with the nymph Pasithea to put Zeus to sleep during the Trojan War
Previously fled to Nyx for protection when Zeus nearly caught him for causing mischief
His cave is filled with poppies and the sound of running water to induce sleep
Father of Morpheus who shapes human dreams

name: Morpheus
title: God of Dreams
description: Morpheus is the god of dreams and the shaper of human forms in dreams. Son of Hypnos (Sleep) and Pasithea. He appears to mortals in dreams disguised as human figures — while his brothers Phobetor takes animal form and Phantasos takes the form of inanimate objects. He is the most powerful of the Oneiroi (dream gods).
personality: Mysterious, silent, fluid. Takes the shape of others and speaks prophecy through dreams.
myths: Sent by Hypnos or the gods to deliver dream messages to mortals
Appeared to Alcyone as her dead husband Ceyx to deliver the news of his death
The word 'morphine' is derived from his name
He and his brothers guard the gate of dreams — the ivory gate (false dreams) and the gate of horn (true dreams)

name: Nemesis
title: Goddess of Retribution
description: Nemesis is the goddess of retribution and divine balance. She punishes excessive pride (hubris), good fortune undeserved, and those who commit crimes against the natural order. She is the daughter of Nyx (Night) and represents the force that ensures no one rises too high or falls too low — the universe's corrective force.
personality: Impartial, relentless, and just. Cannot be escaped. Her name literally means 'to give what is due.'
powers: Assigns fortune and misfortune to balance the universe
myths: Punished Narcissus for his cruelty to those who loved him — cursed him to fall in love with his own reflection
Chased by Zeus who wanted to mate with her — she transformed into many animals but he caught her
In some myths she is the true mother of Helen of Troy
No mortal or god can escape her eventual justice — even the powerful must eventually face balance

name: Thanatos
title: God of Death
description: Thanatos is the personification of peaceful death. Twin brother of Hypnos (Sleep) and son of Nyx (Night). He is distinct from Hades — Thanatos is the act of dying itself, not the ruler of the dead. He is gentle and non-violent, representing the peaceful end of life. He is nearly always depicted as a winged young man.
personality: Gentle, melancholic, inevitable. Not evil — simply the natural end of mortal life.
powers: Guides souls to the underworld at the moment of death
myths: Sisyphus chained Thanatos so that no mortals could die — the gods had to send Ares to free him
Alcestis volunteered to die in place of her husband Admetus — Heracles wrestled Thanatos and won her back
He and his brother Hypnos carried the body of Sarpedon from the Trojan battlefield at Zeus's command

name: Pan
title: God of the Wild
description: Pan is the god of the wild, shepherds, flocks, and rustic music. Son of Hermes, he has the hindquarters, legs, and horns of a goat. He roams the wilderness of Arcadia, playing his pan flute. His sudden appearances could cause irrational fear in both animals and people — hence the word 'panic.' He is associated with male sexuality and fertility.
home: The forests and mountains of Arcadia
personality: Lustful, playful, musical, and unpredictable. Delighted in startling travelers. Deeply connected to nature's wild and untamed aspects.
myths: Chased the nymph Syrinx who was transformed into reeds — he made the first pan flute from them
Competed with Apollo in a music contest judged by King Midas — Midas preferred Pan, earning donkey ears
Warned the Athenians before the Battle of Marathon, promising to panic the Persians
His death cry 'The Great Pan is dead!' was said to be heard across the sea in the time of Emperor Tiberius
Helped Zeus defeat Typhon by leaping into the Nile disguised as a fish
